details.diagnosis: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism for the provided molecule (C58H40N2S) using available structure context and first-round internal evidence. Leading working hypothesis: AIE primarily arises from restriction of intramolecular motions (RIM), i.e., aggregation/solid-state constraints reduce phenyl-rotor and torsional vibrations that otherwise open nonradiative internal conversion channels in solution. This is only an initial hypothesis because mechanism depends on excited-state topography (whether torsions lead to S1 minima with low oscillator strength or approach conical intersections), and on aggregate packing (whether excimer/aggregate emissive states form), none of which are confirmed yet. Visible uncertainties: despite high planarity_proxy (~0.97) the molecule still has ~10 rotatable bonds; it is unclear which specific torsions couple most strongly to S1 decay and whether the dominant deactivation is via torsional motion, vibrational reorganization, or aggregate-state formation. Shared prepared structure context is available (ready) with 3D files and a selected conformer, enabling quick microscopic baseline calculations without expensive conformer searches. Specialized agents boundary: Macro agent can analyze structural features/proxies and compare plausible AIE scenarios qualitatively; Microscopic agent can run low-cost Amesp aTB S0 optimization and bounded S1 vertical excitation on the provided conformer(s), but cannot do exhaustive solid-state packing, large-scale TDDFT screening, or full excited-state relaxation/CI searches in the first round. The microscopic baseline must remain low-cost because the molecule is large (101 atoms) and heavy workflows (multi-conformer excited-state optimizations) would be slow and unnecessary before we confirm basic S1 character/oscillator strength sensitivity. First round should gather both macro and microscopic evidence: macro to frame plausible motion/packing mechanisms from structure; microscopic to obtain quick S0/S1 indicators (transition character, oscillator strength, geometry-change sensitivity) that can support or weaken the RIM hypothesis.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Low-cost baseline S0/S1 evidence collection only. Reuse prepared_xyz_path/prepared_sdf_path and selected_conformer_id=5. Steps: (1) run Amesp aTB S0 geometry optimization on the selected conformer; record final energy, key dihedrals for the main rotatable bonds (report 3–6 representative torsions), and any major planarization/twisting. (2) compute bounded S1 vertical excitation at the S0-optimized geometry with amesp_s1_nstates=5; report S1 energy (eV), oscillator strength, dominant orbital transition description (e.g., HOMO->LUMO contribution if available), and whether the excitation is localized vs delocalized qualitatively from the output. (3) Optional but bounded: generate up to 2 torsion-perturbed snapshots by rotating one or two highest-impact dihedrals (e.g., ±30–45°) without full re-optimization; rerun single-point S1 vertical excitations to see sensitivity of f and S1 energy to torsion. Do not perform full excited-state optimization or extensive conformer search; keep total snapshots ≤3 geometries.

details.task_understanding: Provide a qualitative, structure-proxy-based assessment of likely AIE mechanism(s) consistent with the current hypothesis (RIM), using the shared_structure_context only. Specifically: infer dominant intramolecular motions from rotatable_bond_count/torsion_candidate_count; explain how aggregation could restrict these and impact nonradiative vs radiative decay; comment on π–π stacking/excimer plausibility from planarity/compactness/principal_span proxies; and propose 2–3 testable predictions that help distinguish RIM from aggregate/excimer emission—without making a final global decision.
details.reasoning_summary:
Structure is a large, highly aromatic, multi-phenyl/aryl system (10 aromatic rings; 101 atoms) with multiple exocyclic C=C linkages connecting aryl rotors and one heteroaromatic (nsnc) unit. The proxy rotatable_bond_count = 10 and torsion_candidate_count = 10 indicate many low-frequency torsions, dominated by aryl–aryl single-bond rotations and torsion around aryl–vinyl (C(sp2)–C(sp2)) link connections (phenyl rotors and “vinyl-link torsions”). These motions plausibly enable internal conversion via nonradiative pathways in dilute solution; aggregation can sterically/viscously hinder them (classic RIM/RIR), reducing nonradiative decay and enhancing emission.

Geometry proxies: very high planarity_proxy (~0.97) suggests the selected conformer is largely conjugated/near-coplanar overall (or dominated by extended planar subsegments), while compactness_proxy (~0.95) suggests a fairly dense/compact shape rather than a very open propeller. The large principal_span_proxy (~156) indicates an overall elongated/extended molecular span, which can favor slip-stacked arrangements rather than tight face-to-face cofacial stacks. High planarity can increase π–π interaction propensity, but high steric bulk/branching (branch_point_count 18; many peripheral phenyls) can frustrate perfect cofacial stacking; thus strong H-aggregate quenching via tight π–π may be moderated, while some degree of π-contact (slipped stacks) and excimer-like states remain plausible but not compelled by proxies alone.

Testable distinctions: If RIM dominates, emission should increase with viscosity (or polymer matrix), show strong suppression of nonradiative decay at low temperature, and correlate with restricted torsions; excimer/aggregate emission would more likely show concentration-dependent spectral new bands/broadening/red-shifts and potentially different lifetimes vs monomer emission.
Capability limit note: Assessment is limited to deterministic interpretation of provided SMILES and scalar proxies (rotatable bonds, torsion candidates, planarity/compactness/span). No explicit packing models, excited-state calculations, or quantitative excimer predictions are performed.
details.execution_plan: Use deterministic_macro_structure_scan to execute the bounded macro workflow with focus areas: Topology-driven motion inventory (locations/types of torsions implied by aromatic-rich, multi-aryl conjugated scaffold)., Proxy-based aggregation consequences (steric hindrance, restricted rotations, slipped vs cofacial stacking likelihood)., Mechanism-discriminating observables (viscosity/temperature/concentration-dependent spectra and lifetimes).. Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

details.task_understanding: The task is to perform a low-cost baseline microscopic study on a selected conformer (ID=5) of a prepared molecular structure. This involves (1) running an aTB S0 geometry optimization on the selected conformer, (2) computing vertical S1 excitations at the optimized S0 geometry with 5 excited states, and (3) optionally generating up to 2 torsion-perturbed snapshots by rotating the highest-impact dihedrals to assess sensitivity of excitation energies and oscillator strengths. The goal is to collect evidence relevant to the RIM hypothesis by analyzing geometry changes, key torsions, and excited-state properties without heavy computations or full excited-state optimizations.
details.reasoning_summary: Given the prepared structure and selected conformer ID, the first step is to run the low-cost baseline bundle that includes S0 geometry optimization and vertical excited-state manifold calculation. This will provide the optimized geometry, final energy, and vertical excitation data needed. To assess torsion sensitivity, we need to identify the highest-impact rotatable dihedrals. Since the rotatable dihedrals are known from the prepared structure context, we can list them and select the top 1 or 2 for snapshot generation. Then, we run torsion snapshot calculations with limited angle offsets (±30–45°) and up to 2 snapshots total, followed by vertical excitation calculations on these snapshots. This approach respects the budget and avoids heavy DFT or full excited-state relaxation. The deliverables include optimized S0 geometry, vertical excitation energies and oscillator strengths, and sensitivity summaries for conformer and torsion perturbations.
details.capability_limit_note: The task is bounded to low-cost aTB baseline calculations and limited torsion snapshot perturbations. Full excited-state relaxation, heavy DFT optimizations, or extensive conformer searches are not supported and thus excluded.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Structure is a large, highly aromatic, multi-phenyl/aryl system (10 aromatic rings; 101 atoms) with multiple exocyclic C=C linkages connecting aryl rotors and one heteroaromatic (nsnc) unit. The proxy rotatable_bond_count = 10 and torsion_candidate_count = 10 indicate many low-frequency torsions, dominated by aryl–aryl single-bond rotations and torsion around aryl–vinyl (C(sp2)–C(sp2)) link connections (phenyl rotors and “vinyl-link torsions”). These motions plausibly enable internal conversion via nonradiative pathways in dilute solution; aggregation can sterically/viscously hinder them (classic RIM/RIR), reducing nonradiative decay and enhancing emission.

Geometry proxies: very high planarity_proxy (~0.97) suggests the selected conformer is largely conjugated/near-coplanar overall (or dominated by extended planar subsegments), while compactness_proxy (~0.95) suggests a fairly dense/compact shape rather than a very open propeller. The large principal_span_proxy (~156) indicates an overall elongated/extended molecular span, which can favor slip-stacked arrangements rather than tight face-to-face cofacial stacks. High planarity can increase π–π interaction propensity, but high steric bulk/branching (branch_point_count 18; many peripheral phenyls) can frustrate perfect cofacial stacking; thus strong H-aggregate quenching via tight π–π may be moderated, while some degree of π-contact (slipped stacks) and excimer-like states remain plausible but not compelled by proxies alone.

Testable distinctions: If RIM dominates, emission should increase with viscosity (or polymer matrix), show strong suppression of nonradiative decay at low temperature, and correlate with restricted torsions; excimer/aggregate emission would more likely show concentration-dependent spectral new bands/broadening/red-shifts and potentially different lifetimes vs monomer emission.
Capability limit note: Assessment is limited to deterministic interpretation of provided SMILES and scalar proxies (rotatable bonds, torsion candidates, planarity/compactness/span). No explicit packing models, excited-state calculations, or quantitative excimer predictions are performed.

Local reasoning summary: The initial step is to run a discovery call to list rotatable dihedrals with constraints to focus on high-relevance, non-peripheral bonds, and preference for aryl–aryl and aryl–vinyl bonds near the nsnc core to align with the RIM hypothesis. Once the stable dihedrals are identified, select the one most central to conjugation for torsion snapshot generation. Generate two torsion snapshots at +45° and -45° offsets from the existing S0-optimized geometry artifact without further geometry optimization to keep cost low. Then run vertical excitation calculations on these snapshots (nstates=5) to obtain excitation energies and oscillator strengths, enabling assessment of torsional sensitivity of brightness and energy. The overall approach balances cost and information gain by avoiding new conformer searches and excited-state optimization while leveraging existing artifacts effectively.

Only low-cost torsion snapshots with vertical excitations from existing optimized geometries can be performed within the current capability and cost budget.

details.diagnosis: Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay. Latest evidence strengthens it: the molecule has many torsional DOFs (10 rotatable bonds) and the low-cost vertical S1 is intrinsically very bright (f≈1.03). A targeted ±45° torsion on a central aryl–vinyl dihedral measurably modulates the excited state (ΔE≈0.15 eV; Δf≈0.27), consistent with torsion-dependent conjugation/brightness and thus with motion-restriction enhancing emission. Key remaining uncertainty: these are isolated-molecule vertical excitations; we still lack direct evidence that aggregation specifically suppresses a nonradiative decay channel (e.g., access to dark/CI geometries) rather than creating a new aggregate/excimer emissive state. Best next action: use Verifier to seek external discrimination signals (spectral shifts/broadening, concentration dependence, lifetime changes) typical of excimer/aggregate emission vs RIM.

details.diagnosis: Verifier evidence is broadly supportive (not conflicting) with the current RIM hypothesis but is generic (no exact-identity hit). Cards rim_viscosity_temp_1, multi_rotor_rim_2, and heteroatom_rim_4 align with our internal evidence: the target has many phenyl-rotor/torsional DOFs (macro: 10 rotatable bonds) and torsion-sensitive brightness (microscopic: oscillator strength spread ~0.266 upon ±45° twist), consistent with motion-gated nonradiative decay in solution that is suppressed upon rigidification/aggregation. The excimer_discrim_3 card does not contradict RIM; it instead sharpens the discriminator criteria (red-shifted broad bands, distinct lifetimes, strong concentration dependence, π–π <4 Å) that we currently cannot test with available internal tools. Conflict is weak-to-none; no hypothesis switch is necessary. Remaining uncertainty is mainly residual and due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than hypothesis weakness; internal agents are capability-limited for explicit aggregate modeling and we have no compound-specific literature evidence. Conservative contraction is appropriate: finalize with RIM as best-supported primary mechanism, while explicitly noting that excimer/aggregate emissive states remain a plausible secondary alternative absent spectroscopic/structural discriminator data.
details.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Finalized as RIM-dominant based on strong structure+torsion-sensitivity support and verifier-aligned mechanistic context, but compound-specific aggregate-state evidence is absent; excimer/aggregate-state emission cannot be fully excluded without concentration-dependent spectra, lifetime changes, and/or packing metrics (e.g., π–π distances).
details.final_hypothesis_rationale: Best-supported mechanism is RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) as the primary driver of AIE for this multi-rotor conjugated luminogen. Internal evidence: (i) macro descriptors show a multi-phenyl scaffold with substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds/torsion candidates), consistent with solution-phase nonradiative decay via low-frequency motions; (ii) microscopic aTB vertical excitations show an intrinsically bright lowest excited state (E~3.06 eV, f~1.03); (iii) targeted torsion snapshots about a central aryl–vinyl dihedral produce meaningful changes in excitation energy (~0.152 eV spread) and oscillator strength (~0.266 spread; f reduced in a twisted snapshot), indicating emission properties are sensitive to torsional motion—supporting torsion-gated nonradiative pathways that aggregation/rigidification can suppress. Verifier evidence supports this interpretation generically: ChemRev (10.1021/acs.chemrev.5b00263) reports hallmark viscosity/temperature dependence with minimal spectral shift for RIM-driven AIE, and related multi-rotor/heteroatom cases (10.1021/acs.jpclett.8b00843; 10.1002/adma.201706711) connect increased rotor freedom to faster nonradiative decay while preserving RIM behavior. Main caveat: verifier also highlights excimer diagnostics (10.1038/ncomms14066); without compound-specific concentration-dependent spectra/lifetimes or crystal packing distances, a secondary excimer/aggregate emissive contribution cannot be ruled out, but current evidence most strongly supports RIM as dominant.
details.capability_assessment: Macro and microscopic agents provided strong single-molecule motion and torsion-sensitivity evidence, but are not equipped for explicit aggregate packing/excimer state modeling or for importing compound-specific experimental spectra/lifetimes; verifier retrieval returned only generic/similar-family context and no exact-identity evidence, so the remaining gap is primarily external-data-limited.

details.current_hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay
details.diagnosis: Verifier evidence is broadly supportive (not conflicting) with the current RIM hypothesis but is generic (no exact-identity hit). Cards rim_viscosity_temp_1, multi_rotor_rim_2, and heteroatom_rim_4 align with our internal evidence: the target has many phenyl-rotor/torsional DOFs (macro: 10 rotatable bonds) and torsion-sensitive brightness (microscopic: oscillator strength spread ~0.266 upon ±45° twist), consistent with motion-gated nonradiative decay in solution that is suppressed upon rigidification/aggregation. The excimer_discrim_3 card does not contradict RIM; it instead sharpens the discriminator criteria (red-shifted broad bands, distinct lifetimes, strong concentration dependence, π–π <4 Å) that we currently cannot test with available internal tools. Conflict is weak-to-none; no hypothesis switch is necessary. Remaining uncertainty is mainly residual and due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than hypothesis weakness; internal agents are capability-limited for explicit aggregate modeling and we have no compound-specific literature evidence. Conservative contraction is appropriate: finalize with RIM as best-supported primary mechanism, while explicitly noting that excimer/aggregate emissive states remain a plausible secondary alternative absent spectroscopic/structural discriminator data.
details.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Finalized as RIM-dominant based on strong structure+torsion-sensitivity support and verifier-aligned mechanistic context, but compound-specific aggregate-state evidence is absent; excimer/aggregate-state emission cannot be fully excluded without concentration-dependent spectra, lifetime changes, and/or packing metrics (e.g., π–π distances).
details.final_hypothesis_rationale: Best-supported mechanism is RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) as the primary driver of AIE for this multi-rotor conjugated luminogen. Internal evidence: (i) macro descriptors show a multi-phenyl scaffold with substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds/torsion candidates), consistent with solution-phase nonradiative decay via low-frequency motions; (ii) microscopic aTB vertical excitations show an intrinsically bright lowest excited state (E~3.06 eV, f~1.03); (iii) targeted torsion snapshots about a central aryl–vinyl dihedral produce meaningful changes in excitation energy (~0.152 eV spread) and oscillator strength (~0.266 spread; f reduced in a twisted snapshot), indicating emission properties are sensitive to torsional motion—supporting torsion-gated nonradiative pathways that aggregation/rigidification can suppress. Verifier evidence supports this interpretation generically: ChemRev (10.1021/acs.chemrev.5b00263) reports hallmark viscosity/temperature dependence with minimal spectral shift for RIM-driven AIE, and related multi-rotor/heteroatom cases (10.1021/acs.jpclett.8b00843; 10.1002/adma.201706711) connect increased rotor freedom to faster nonradiative decay while preserving RIM behavior. Main caveat: verifier also highlights excimer diagnostics (10.1038/ncomms14066); without compound-specific concentration-dependent spectra/lifetimes or crystal packing distances, a secondary excimer/aggregate emissive contribution cannot be ruled out, but current evidence most strongly supports RIM as dominant.
details.capability_assessment: Macro and microscopic agents provided strong single-molecule motion and torsion-sensitivity evidence, but are not equipped for explicit aggregate packing/excimer state modeling or for importing compound-specific experimental spectra/lifetimes; verifier retrieval returned only generic/similar-family context and no exact-identity evidence, so the remaining gap is primarily external-data-limited.
details.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation observed; each round reduced uncertainty (established bright monomer, then demonstrated torsion-sensitive brightness). The remaining gap is not shrinking further due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than ineffective iteration.
details.contraction_reason: Finalize with a conservative statement: RIM is best supported by internal calculations and verifier-aligned mechanistic context; excimer/aggregate emission remains a secondary possibility absent discriminator measurements. Further internal work would be low-yield given current tool limits.
